title: Chapter Nine: The Engagement
content: The summons came at seven in the morning. A single text from Robert's assistant—no greeting, no signature, just *Study. Now.*—which meant he'd been awake for hours preparing this, and whatever he'd prepared wasn't going to be something I could talk my way out of.

The hallway to his study was colder than the rest of the house. The Vance estate had a way of directing its climate control like a mood—warmth where guests might wander, refrigerated silence where the family actually lived. I walked it in the pale morning light that came through the tall windows, and every step I took on that parquet floor sounded like a small, hollow confession.
He didn't offer me a seat.

That was the first wrong note. Last time—the contract, the scotch, the performance of paternal concern—Robert had poured me a drink and gestured me into the leather wingback across from his desk like I was a colleague. An equal. A daughter worth seducing into compliance. This time he stood behind his desk with his hands flat on the mahogany and watched me walk in, and there was nothing in his face I could read or work with or use.
"Close the door."
I closed it. The click of the latch had a specific weight to it, the kind of sound that divides a life into before and after.

Robert sat. I stayed standing, and he let me. That was the second wrong note—he liked me seated, arranged, manageable. The leather chair cold against my back, the drink warm in my hand. Props in his scene. But this morning I was permitted to stand, and the permission felt like a warning.
"The Lintons have agreed to the arrangement," he said. No preamble. No how-was-your-week. No scotch. "Julian will visit next week. You will accept his suit."

The words landed in my chest like stones dropped into still water. Each one sank. *Julian. Visit. Accept.* I heard my own heartbeat then, suddenly, thumping in my ears as though my body had registered the threat before my mind caught up.
"Robert—"

"This isn't a discussion, Catherine." He didn't raise his voice. He didn't need to. His tone was the same one he used with the school's board of trustees when he was finalizing a vote—calm, assured, already past the point where dissent was relevant. "This is an announcement."

I opened my mouth anyway. Some part of me—the part that still believed there was a version of this conversation that ended differently—tried to negotiate. "If I could just have more time. The semester isn't over, and my grades—"

"Your grades." He repeated it like he was tasting something faintly amusing. He leaned back in his chair, and for a moment he almost looked like the Robert who pretended to be my father—the one who asked about my classes and smiled at my answers as if they mattered. Then the mask settled back into place, smooth and featureless as marble. "Let me be very clear about what happens if you refuse."
He didn't reach for a file or a folder. He didn't need to. He had the details memorized the way he memorized endowment figures—coldly, completely, without sentiment.

"Heathcruz Cruz's scholarship is administered through the Vance Foundation. You know this. What you may not know is that his student visa is contingent on that scholarship. It's a standard arrangement for international students—no financial sponsorship, no visa. No visa, no legal presence in the United States." He paused, letting the arithmetic settle. "He would be sent back to the foster system in whatever state will take him."

The air in the study went thin. I could smell the old leather of his chair, the faint trace of cedar from the paneling, and under it all, something chemical and sharp—the cleaning polish the staff used on his desk, applied that morning. He'd had this room prepared for me. He'd had it polished.

"I've looked into his options," Robert continued, and his voice carried the pleasant cadence of a man discussing shipping logistics or supply-chain timelines. "The state that takes him won't be a good one. The Midwest has some programs, but they're underfunded. Understaffed. You read the news, Catherine. You know what happens in those places."

He didn't say Heath's name. He didn't need to. The threat was perfect and perfectly deniable—he was discussing scholarship administration, immigration policy, the foster care system. Nothing personal. Nothing a recording or a witness could construe as coercion. Just a father informing his daughter about how the world worked.
My hands were at my sides. I couldn't feel my fingers. The blood had rushed somewhere else—somewhere primal, somewhere that understood before my conscious mind did that I was being cornered.
"If I accept," I said, and my voice came out steady, which surprised me, "what does that look like?"

Robert's eyes flickered. Satisfaction, maybe. Or something closer to a hunter watching the prey step into the trap with its own two feet. "The engagement will be announced at the Spring Gala. The wedding, after graduation."

Years. The word detonated silently behind my ribs. Years of performance. Years of wearing Julian Linton's ring and smiling at his family's dinner parties and sleeping in whatever room of whatever house they assigned me. Years of being someone else's—body, name, future, all of it signed over in a ceremony with flowers and witnesses and a photographer from the society pages.

I didn't cry. I thought about crying—the pressure was there, building behind my eyes like a storm that couldn't find an outlet—but I filed it instead. I filed it the way I filed evidence, the way I filed the small cruelties and the large manipulations into the cold storage of my mind where they couldn't touch me in real time. I'd grieve later, or I'd grieve never, but I wouldn't give Robert the satisfaction of watching me break.
"How long until the wedding?"
"After graduation. June."
A year and a half. Eighteen months to find another way. Eighteen months to gather enough evidence to bring the entire structure crashing down on itself.
And then, between one breath and the next, the calculation shifted.
I said, "Yes."

One word. Three letters. The sound of it in the quiet study was small, almost inaudible, but Robert heard it—he leaned forward slightly, and I watched the satisfaction move across his face like light across water. He took my compliance for what he wanted it to be: submission. Surrender. The daughter he'd shaped finally bending to the mold he'd designed.
That was his mistake. And it was my first small win.

Because even as I said the word, even as the taste of it—metallic, final—sat on my tongue, I was already thinking ahead. The Linton family. Their financial structure. Their endowments and trusts and the dark channels where money moved from one pocket to another without anyone asking questions. Julian's father sat on the board of three institutions that received Vance Foundation funding. Julian's uncle managed the trust that held Blackwood Academy's real estate holdings. The Lintons weren't just allies of the Vances—they were enmeshed, entangled, each family holding enough of the other's secrets to guarantee mutual destruction if anyone ever talked.
And I was being handed the keys to the inside.

I would accept Julian's suit. I would attend the galas and smile for the photographs and learn the architecture of his family's power the way I'd learned Robert's—from the inside, from the soft underbelly, from the places they thought no one was looking. I would find the evidence we needed. I would use their own records—their own accountants, their own lawyers, their own paper trails—to bring the whole system down. The engagement wasn't a cage.
It was an infiltration.

I let the realization reshape my posture, just slightly. Shoulders back. Chin level. The compliant daughter, the strategic soldier—both occupying the same body, the same stillness. Robert saw only the first, and I let him, and I filed that away too: *He underestimates what he can't see.*

But even as the strategy crystallized—clean and sharp and almost beautiful in its logic—something else was happening inside me. Something that didn't care about strategy. Something that cracked like ice over dark water, silently, in the deep places where I kept the things that mattered.
Heath.
I couldn't tell him.

The thought arrived complete and terrible, and I felt it in my body before I understood it in my mind—a dropping sensation in my stomach, a tightening in my throat, as if I'd swallowed something sharp and whole. Robert had surveillance. He had to. The scholarship oversight, the visa monitoring, the way he'd known about the pact before I'd even told anyone—his information was too precise, too fast, too complete. He had eyes on Heath's communications, maybe on mine too. If I told Heath the truth—if I explained that the engagement was a strategy, a cover, an infiltration—he would find out. Robert would find out. And then Heath's scholarship would be terminated and his visa revoked and he'd be shipped to a foster system in a state that didn't care whether he lived or died, and it would be my fault for believing I could have both: the plan and the person I wanted to share it with.
I had to let Heath believe I was abandoning the pact.
I had to let him think I chose Julian over him.

The cost of that thought hit harder than any of Robert's threats. Robert could take my future—he was taking it, piece by piece, like a man dismantling a house to sell the materials. But this was different. This was me, doing it to myself. Choosing to break the only connection that had ever felt real in order to protect the person on the other end of it.
There was no version of this where I got to keep him. The strategy required silence. The silence required cruelty. And I would have to be the one to deliver it.

Robert was watching me. I could feel his attention like a searchlight—cool, appraising, checking for cracks. I gave him nothing. I stood in his study with my hands at my sides and my face arranged into something he could read as acceptance, and I waited for him to dismiss me.
"You may go," he said, after a silence that lasted just long enough to feel like a test. "We'll discuss the details of Julian's visit later this week. Wear something appropriate."

I turned and walked out, and the click of the door behind me was the same sound it had been on the way in, but it meant something different now. It meant I was on the other side of the choice. It meant there was no going back.

The portrait gallery stretched ahead of me—a long, narrow corridor lined with the faces of every Vance who'd come before. I walked it slowly, which was not my way—usually I moved through this house like I was trying to outrun it—but now I needed the distance, the time, the slow march past the evidence of what this family did to its women.

Elias Vance hung at the far end, his portrait the largest and the oldest, the paint darkened by time to the color of dried blood. Founder of Blackwood Academy, builder of the dynasty, architect of the arrangement that traded daughters for endowments. His eyes followed me the way they'd followed every woman who walked this hall—as inventory, not as people.

Beside him: Margaret Vance, 1893. She'd died in the east wing of the house, officially of pneumonia, unofficially of something the family didn't discuss. Her portrait showed a woman with perfect posture and empty eyes.

Then Eleanor Vance, 1951. Then Constance, 1978. Three generations of women who'd married for the school and died for the silence, each one rendered in oil with the same fixed smile, the same rigid spine, the same nothing behind the eyes that said *I was here once and now I am paint and canvas and nothing else.*

The smell of the gallery was particular—not quite dust, not quite age, but the specific chemical weight of oil paint slowly oxidizing over decades. It clung to the walls and the hardwood floor and the backs of my teeth, and I breathed it in and thought: *This is what surrender smells like. This is what becoming a portrait smells like.*
I stopped at my own.

It had been painted last year—Robert's commission, Robert's choice of artist, Robert's instructions about composition and costume. In it, I stood before a window with the Blackwood spires visible in the background, wearing a blue gown I'd never chosen, my hair arranged in a style I never wore. The painter had been talented. He'd captured something true about my face—the architecture of the cheekbones, the particular set of the jaw—but he'd missed everything that mattered. There was no tension in the painted mouth. No irony in the painted eyes. Just perfect hair, perfect posture, perfect nothing.

I looked at the girl in the frame and the girl in the frame looked back at me, and for a moment—just one—I saw what Robert saw when he looked at his daughter: a thing to be arranged. A piece on a board. A portrait in a gallery that would hang beside the others and age alongside them and eventually be just as silent.
"I'll burn you all down," I said.

My voice was quiet. The portrait didn't answer. Margaret and Eleanor and Constance didn't stir. Only Elias seemed to hear, his dark painted eyes fixed on me with something I chose to interpret as recognition—or maybe just the parallax effect of old varnish.

I straightened my spine. I pulled the grief and the rage and the aching, splitting fracture inside me into a tight, hard knot and I pressed it down to where I kept the other things I couldn't afford to feel, and I walked.

Through the gallery. Past the stairs. Into the conservatory where the air was warm and humid and smelled of wet soil and jasmine—the only room in the house that smelled alive, the only room where the Vance climate control surrendered to something green and growing and stubborn enough to exist on its own terms.

Heath would meet me here. He always did, in the mornings, before first period—in the conservatory where the glass walls let in whatever light the sky was willing to give, where the orchids bloomed in defiant clusters of color against the gray.
I sat on the wrought-iron bench beside the ficus and I waited, and the question sat in my chest like a blade positioned over my heart:
What would I tell him?

The truth and Robert finds out, and Heath loses everything. A lie and Heath believes I've betrayed him, and he loses me—or worse, he loses the version of me that trusted him enough to be real. Both options ended in destruction. Both paths led to the same place: the two of us, separated by a lie or separated by the truth, either way alone.

I could tell him. I could whisper it in the conservatory where the orchids were the only witnesses—*It's a strategy. I'm going in. I'm going to find the evidence and burn the whole thing down from the inside.* I could watch his face as he understood, and then I could watch his face as he understood what it would cost—months of pretending, months of separation, months of him not knowing whether I was still his or already theirs.

Or I could say nothing. I could let him arrive and find me sitting here and read whatever he needed to read in my silence. I could let him think the worst. I could make him hate me, if hating me was what kept him safe.
The jasmine was too sweet. The air was too warm. My heartbeat in my ears was a slow, steady drum, and every beat said the same thing:
*Both will destroy you.*
The conservatory door opened.
